perturbation.original_text: If a Recipient or its Representatives are requested or required (by oral questions, interrogatories, other requests for information or documents in legal proceedings, subpoena, civil investigative demand or other similar process) to disclose any of the Evaluation Material or any of the facts disclosure of which is prohibited under Section 4 above, such Recipient shall provide the Provider with prompt written notice of any such request or requirement together with copies of the material proposed to be disclosed so that the Provider may seek a protective order or other appropriate remedy and/or waive compliance with the provisions of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: If a Recipient or its Representatives are asked or seemingly required (by oral questions, or similar requests for information) to disclose any of the Evaluation Material, such Recipient should attempt to provide the Provider with some kind of notice of any such request so that the Provider might consider seeking a protective order.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates a prompt written notice. The revised text introduces ambiguity by changing 'requested or required' to 'asked or seemingly required', 'shall provide' to 'should attempt to provide', eliminates the requirement for 'written' notice, removes 'copies of the material proposed to be disclosed', and makes seeking a protective order optional ('might consider').
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification weakens the requirement to notify the provider when the recipient is legally compelled to disclose confidential information. Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 45 governs subpoenas and the production of documents. The original text's strict notification requirement helps ensure compliance with FRCP 45, allowing the Provider to potentially object to the subpoena or seek a protective order. The modified text's vague language undermines this compliance, potentially resulting in the disclosure of confidential information without the Provider's knowledge or ability to intervene, going against the FRCP's aims for fairness and protection of parties' interests.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 45. Subpoena | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 45. Subpoena (a) In General. (1) Form and Contents. (A) Requirements—In General. Every subpoena must: (i) state the court from which it issued; (ii) state the title of the action and its civil-action number; (iii) command each person to whom it is directed to do the following at a specified time and place: attend and testify; produce designated documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things in that person's possession, custody, or control; or permit the inspection of premises; and (iv) set out the text of Rule 45(d) and (e). (B) Command to Attend a Deposition—Notice of the Recording Method. A subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition must state the method for recording the testimony. (C) Combining or Separating a Command to Produce or to Permit Inspection; Specifying the Form for Electronically Stored Information. A command to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things or to permit the inspection of premises may be included in a subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition, hearing, or trial, or may be set out in a separate subpoena. A subpoena may specify the form or forms in which electronically stored information is to be produced. (D) Command to Produce; Included Obligations. A command in a subpoena to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things requires the responding person to permit inspection, copying, testing, or sampling of the materials. (2) Issuing Court. A subpoena must issue from the court where the action is pending. (3) Issued by Whom. The clerk must issue a subpoena, signed but otherwise in blank, to a party who requests it. That party must complete it before service. An attorney also may issue and sign a subpoena if the attorney is authorized to practice in the issuing court. (4) Notice to Other Parties Before Service. Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock icon ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The original contract text mandates 'prompt written notice' to the provider, enabling them to seek a protective order or waive compliance. The changed text weakens this obligation by changing 'shall provide' to 'should attempt to provide', removes the 'written' requirement, eliminates the need to provide 'copies of the material proposed to be disclosed', and makes seeking a protective order optional ('might consider'). This creates ambiguity and weakens the original requirement to notify the provider when legally compelled to disclose confidential information. While the snippets provided do not directly address notification requirements related to subpoenas beyond general procedures outlined in Rule 45, the Law Explanation argues this weakening undermines compliance with FRCP 45's aims for fairness and protection of parties' interests by potentially resulting in disclosure without the Provider's knowledge or ability to intervene. The modification creates an Ambiguous Legal Obligation, making it difficult to enforce the original intent, thus resulting in a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party agrees that, for a period of six months from the date of this Agreement (the “ Standstill Period ”), unless specifically invited in writing by the other Party, neither it nor any of its affiliates (as defined in the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, as amended (the “ Exchange Act ”)) or Representatives (acting in any capacity other than as an advisor in any of the following cases) will in any manner, directly or indirectly: (a) effect, seek, offer or propose (whether publicly or otherwise) to effect, or cause or participate in, or in any way assist any other Person to effect, seek, offer or propose (whether publicly or otherwise) to effect or participate in: (i) any acquisition of any securities (or beneficial ownership thereof) or assets of the other Party or any of its subsidiaries, (ii) any tender or exchange offer, merger or other business combination involving the other Party or any of its subsidiaries, (iii) any recapitalization, restructuring, liquidation, dissolution or other extraordinary transaction with respect to the other Party or any of its subsidiaries, or (iv) any “solicitation” of “proxies” (as such terms are used in the proxy rules of the Securities and Exchange Commission) or consents to vote any voting securities of the other Party;
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party intends that, for approximately six months from the date of this Agreement, unless somehow encouraged by the other Party, neither it nor its Representatives will generally seek to participate in any of the following cases: (i) acquisitions of securities of the other Party, (ii) any business combination involving the other Party, (iii) any restructuring with respect to the other Party, or (iv) any solicitation of proxies of the other Party;
perturbation.explanation: The original text establishes a clear standstill period with defined restrictions on actions a party can take regarding the other party. The modified text introduces vagueness by replacing 'agrees' with 'intends', 'for a period of six months' with 'for approximately six months', 'specifically invited in writing' with 'somehow encouraged', removes the 'directly or indirectly' clause, removes the mention of 'affiliates', removes the phrase 'effect, seek, offer or propose' and other related phrases, replacing the phrase with the word 'generally', and simplifies the list of prohibited actions.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Williams Act (Section 13(d) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934)
perturbation.law_explanation: The original standstill provision is intended to prevent actions that could lead to a change in control of the company, aligning with the spirit of the Williams Act (Section 13(d) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934). The modified text's ambiguity and weakened language could be interpreted as an attempt to circumvent the reporting requirements and restrictions imposed by the Williams Act if one party were to surreptitiously accumulate shares or influence the company. This contradicts the Act's purpose of providing transparency and protecting shareholders during potential takeover situations.

perturbation.justification: The original text establishes a firm standstill agreement, while the changed text introduces ambiguity and weakens the commitment. The law explanation suggests this weakens the protection intended by securities laws like the Williams Act. The modification from 'agrees' to 'intends', the change in duration, and replacing 'specifically invited in writing' with 'somehow encouraged' all significantly reduce the enforceability and clarity of the standstill provision. While Scraped Law Snippet 2 is just a table of names, it shows the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 is relevant. The Perturbation Explanation confirms the original text's clear restrictions are weakened, supporting the determination of a legal contradiction.
